findings.evidence_quote:
File for $0 on the app
Switch to TurboTax, do it yourself and file for free on the app by 2/28. See offer details
findings.location: SWITCH TO TURBOTAX section, 'File for $0 on the app' hero banner
findings.reasoning: Trigger: The page explicitly promotes filing 'for free' / 'for $0 on the app', which is a mention of TurboTax Free. This triggers R-01's requirement that the ~37% filer eligibility disclosure with the simple Form 1040 limitation must be disclosed right underneath the free mention. Requirement analysis: Nowhere on the page — not underneath this banner, not in the 'Offer Details' section, not in the footer — does the required '~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only (no schedules, except for EITC, CTC, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A)' disclosure appear. The 'See offer details' link leads to an Offer Details section that only discusses the 15%-off discount promotion terms (referral link, filing deadline, exclusions), not the Free Edition eligibility disclosure. Axis A: not compliant because the required disclosure is entirely absent. Axis B: since no version of the disclosure exists anywhere on the page, there is no wording to compare against the approved library text, so it necessarily fails to match (false) rather than na, since the rule is triggered and the omission itself is a defect, not merely a wording drift. Strongest counter-reading: one could argue this 'file for free' offer refers to the discount/promotional free filing (via app by 2/28) rather than the 'TurboTax Free Edition' product per se, potentially making the disclosure not required. However, the rule's trigger is broad ('If Turbotax free is mentioned') and does not carve out promotional free offers from paid discount offers; any assertion of $0/free filing plausibly invokes the same consumer-protection concern the disclosure is meant to address (that only ~37% qualify for free simple-return filing). Given the ambiguity about whether this specific $0-on-app promotion is the same as the standard Free Edition product, I still flag it as a violation because the page provides no eligibility caveat at all, and erring toward requiring disclosure protects consumers from an unqualified 'free' claim.

findings.evidence_quote: File today to get a 15% off discount and your biggest possible refund. See Offer Details
findings.location: Hero banner 'Refund Szn is here!' section
findings.reasoning: Trigger analysis: this specific hero banner references a 15%-off discount, not literally 'free,' so in isolation it would not trigger R-01. However the page as a whole also markets a distinct $0/free-on-app offer, and the footer link list includes 'Free Edition tax filing.' Because the rule specifies 'if TurboTax free is mentioned,' and the phrase 'Free Edition tax filing' appears as a footer navigation link, this is a mention of the Free product. Requirement: the disclosure ('~37% of filers qualify...') does not appear directly underneath or near this link, nor anywhere else on the page. Axis A: not compliant. Axis B: false, since the required text is absent entirely. Counter-reading considered: a mere navigational link labeled 'Free Edition tax filing' pointing to another page might be considered too incidental/generic to count as a 'mention' triggering the on-page disclosure requirement, since the actual eligibility details reside on the destination page itself. This is a plausible reading under which the rule would not trigger from a footer nav link. Given genuine ambiguity about whether generic footer navigation labels invoking product names count as 'mentions' requiring an on-this in-page disclosure, this determination is a judgment call best flagged for human policy clarification.

Scanned the full page text for any mention of "TurboTax Free", "Free Edition", or equivalent explicit naming of the TurboTax Free product. The only potentially-related phrases are generic calls to action like "Start for Free" and promotional copy such as "File for $0 on the app" and a footer navigation link labeled "Free Edition tax filing", but none of these explicitly say "TurboTax free" (the product name) as required by the rule trigger. The rule R-01 is keyed specifically to mentions of "Turbotax free"; generic free/0$ language or a navigational anchor text "Free Edition tax filing" does not clearly qualify as that product mention under a strict reading. Therefore the IF condition is not met, the rule does not apply, and no eligibility disclosure is required here. Strongest counter-reading is that "Free Edition tax filing" in the footer could be interpreted as a reference to TurboTax Free; however, R-01 is keyed to the phrase "Turbotax free" and compliance practice treats that separately from the Free Edition navigation link, so I rejected that broader interpretation.

The page mentions TurboTax free (“Start for Free”), which triggers R-01. There is no immediately adjacent disclosure stating “~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only (no schedules, except for EITC, CTC, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A).” No such disclosure appears elsewhere on the page either. Axis A fails (no required disclosure), and Axis B fails (no match to the approved wording). Strongest counter-reading: “Start for Free” could mean beginning without payment and not the TurboTax Free product. However, R-01 applies when TurboTax free is mentioned in marketing; without the required eligibility disclosure, it is non-compliant.

Explicit free-file claim triggers R-01. There is no disclosure right underneath or anywhere nearby providing the required eligibility language. Offer Details linked in this section also do not contain the required disclosure. Therefore Axis A fails and Axis B fails. Counter-reading that this is a time-bound promo with separate terms does not satisfy R-01’s mandated Free eligibility disclosure.

Direct mention of TurboTax Free Edition triggers R-01. No eligibility disclosure appears right underneath this mention or elsewhere in the footer on this page. Thus Axis A fails and Axis B fails. While one might argue a mere navigational link is exempt, the rule’s trigger is any mention on the page; absent the required adjacent disclosure, it is non-compliant.
